title: Drug withdrawal

infobox.value: – a [neuropsychological](/wiki/Neuropsychological "Neuropsychological") disorder characterized by a persistent and intense urge to use a drug or engage in a behavior that produces natural reward * **[addictive drug](/wiki/Addiction "Addiction")** – psychoactive substances that with repeated use are associated with significantly higher rates of substance use disorders, due in large part to the drug's effect on brain [reward systems](/wiki/Reward_system "Reward system") * **[dependence](/wiki/Substance_dependence "Substance dependence")** – an adaptive state associated with a withdrawal syndrome upon cessation of repeated exposure to a stimulus (e.g., drug intake) * **[drug sensitization](/wiki/Reverse_tolerance "Reverse tolerance")** or **reverse tolerance** – the escalating effect of a drug resulting from repeated administration at a given dose * **drug withdrawal** – symptoms that occur upon cessation of repeated drug use * **[physical dependence](/wiki/Physical_dependence "Physical dependence")** – dependence that involves persistent physical–[somatic](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/somatic "wikt:somatic") withdrawal symptoms (e.g., [delirium tremens](/wiki/Delirium_tremens "Delirium tremens") and [nausea](/wiki/Nausea "Nausea")) * **[psychological dependence](/wiki/Psychological_dependence "Psychological dependence")** – dependence that is characterised by emotional-motivational withdrawal symptoms (e.g., [anhedonia](/wiki/Anhedonia "Anhedonia") and [anxiety](/wiki/Anxiety "Anxiety")) that affect cognitive functioning. * **[reinforcing](/wiki/Reinforcement "Reinforcement") stimuli** – stimuli that increase the probability of repeating behaviors paired with them * **[rewarding](/wiki/Reward_system "Reward system") stimuli** – stimuli that the brain interprets as intrinsically positive and desirable or as something to approach * **[sensitization](/wiki/Sensitization "Sensitization")** – an amplified response to a stimulus resulting from repeated exposure to it * **[substance use disorder](/wiki/Substance_use_disorder "Substance use disorder")** – a condition in which the use of substances leads to clinically and functionally significant impairment or distress * **[drug tolerance](/wiki/Drug_tolerance "Drug tolerance")** – the diminishing effect of a drug resulting from repeated administration at a given dose | |

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Medical condition caused by drug addiction

"Pink Cloud" redirects here. For the EP, see [*Pink Cloud* (EP)](/wiki/Pink_Cloud_(EP) "Pink Cloud (EP)").

Medical condition

| Drug withdrawal | |
| --- | --- |
| [Specialty](/wiki/Medical_specialty "Medical specialty") | [Psychiatry](/wiki/Psychiatry "Psychiatry") |

**Drug withdrawal**, **drug withdrawal syndrome**, or **substance withdrawal syndrome**[[1]](#cite_note-MeSH-1) is the group of [symptoms](/wiki/Symptom "Symptom") that occur upon the abrupt discontinuation or decrease in the intake of [pharmaceutical](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_drug "Pharmaceutical drug") or [recreational drugs](/wiki/Recreational_drug_use "Recreational drug use").

In order for the symptoms of withdrawal to occur, one must have first developed a form of [drug dependence](/wiki/Substance_dependence "Substance dependence"). This may occur as [physical dependence](/wiki/Physical_dependence "Physical dependence"), [psychological dependence](/wiki/Psychological_dependence "Psychological dependence"), or both. Drug dependence develops from consuming one or more substances over a period of time.

Dependence arises in a [dose-dependent](/wiki/Dose-dependent "Dose-dependent") manner and produces withdrawal symptoms that vary with the type of drug that is consumed. For example, prolonged use of an [antidepressant](/wiki/Antidepressant "Antidepressant") medication is likely to cause a rather different reaction when [discontinued](/wiki/Antidepressant_discontinuation_syndrome "Antidepressant discontinuation syndrome") compared to discontinuation of an [opioid](/wiki/Opioid "Opioid"), such as [heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin"). Withdrawal symptoms from opiates include joint, bone and muscle pain, anxiety, cold sweats, increased heart rate and blood pressure, dilated pupils, vomiting, and diarrhea. Alcohol withdrawal symptoms include irritability, fatigue, shaking, sweating, and nausea. Withdrawal from [nicotine](/wiki/Nicotine "Nicotine") can cause irritability, fatigue, insomnia, headache, and difficulty concentrating. Many prescription and legal nonprescription substances can also cause withdrawal symptoms when individuals stop consuming them, even if they were taken as directed by a physician.

The route of administration, whether [intravenous](/wiki/Intravenous_drug_use "Intravenous drug use"), [intramuscular](/wiki/Intramuscular_injection "Intramuscular injection"), [oral](/wiki/Oral_drug "Oral drug"), or otherwise, can also play a role in determining the severity of withdrawal symptoms.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] There are different stages of withdrawal as well; generally, a person will start to feel bad ([crash](/wiki/Comedown_(drugs) "Comedown (drugs)") or comedown), progress to feeling worse, hit a plateau, and then the symptoms begin to dissipate. However, withdrawal from certain drugs ([barbiturates](/wiki/Barbiturates "Barbiturates"), [benzodiazepines](/wiki/Benzodiazepines "Benzodiazepines"), [alcohol](/wiki/Alcohol_(drug) "Alcohol (drug)"), [glucocorticoids](/wiki/Glucocorticoids "Glucocorticoids")) can be fatal.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) While it is seldom fatal to the user, withdrawal from opiates (and some other drugs) can cause cardiac arrest in some cases, and miscarriages due to fetal withdrawal. The term "[cold turkey](/wiki/Cold_turkey "Cold turkey")" is used to describe the sudden cessation of use of a substance and the ensuing physiologic manifestations.

The symptoms from withdrawal may be even more dramatic when the drug has masked prolonged [malnutrition](/wiki/Malnutrition "Malnutrition"), disease, [chronic pain](/wiki/Chronic_pain "Chronic pain"), infections (common in intravenous drug use), or [sleep deprivation](/wiki/Sleep_deprivation "Sleep deprivation"), conditions that drug abusers often develop as a secondary consequence of the drug. When the drug is removed, these conditions may resurface and be confused with withdrawal symptoms. Genes that encode for the [alpha5 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor](/wiki/Alpha5_nicotinic_acetylcholine_receptor "Alpha5 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor") affect nicotine and alcohol withdrawal symptoms.

## Effect on homeostasis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drug_withdrawal&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Effect on homeostasis")]

[Homeostasis](/wiki/Homeostasis "Homeostasis") is the body's ability to maintain a certain chemical equilibrium in the brain and throughout the body. For example, the function of [shivering](/wiki/Shivering "Shivering") in response to cold is to produce heat maintaining internal temperature at around 37 °C (98.6 °F). Homeostasis is impacted in many ways by drug usage and withdrawal. The internal systems perpetuate homeostasis by using different counter-regulatory methods in order to create a new state of balance based on the presence of the drug in the system.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) These methods include adapting the body's levels of neurotransmitters, hormones, and other substances present to adjust for the addition of the drug to the body.[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5)

## Substances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drug_withdrawal&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Substances")]

| Addiction and dependence glossary[[6]](#cite_note-Addiction_glossary-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Cellular_basis-7)[[8]](#cite_note-Brain_disease-8) | |
| --- | --- |
| * **[addiction](/wiki/Addiction "Addiction")** – a [neuropsychological](/wiki/Neuropsychological "Neuropsychological") disorder characterized by a persistent and intense urge to use a drug or engage in a behavior that produces natural reward * **[addictive drug](/wiki/Addiction "Addiction")** – psychoactive substances that with repeated use are associated with significantly higher rates of substance use disorders, due in large part to the drug's effect on brain [reward systems](/wiki/Reward_system "Reward system") * **[dependence](/wiki/Substance_dependence "Substance dependence")** – an adaptive state associated with a withdrawal syndrome upon cessation of repeated exposure to a stimulus (e.g., drug intake) * **[drug sensitization](/wiki/Reverse_tolerance "Reverse tolerance")** or **reverse tolerance** – the escalating effect of a drug resulting from repeated administration at a given dose * **drug withdrawal** – symptoms that occur upon cessation of repeated drug use * **[physical dependence](/wiki/Physical_dependence "Physical dependence")** – dependence that involves persistent physical–[somatic](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/somatic "wikt:somatic") withdrawal symptoms (e.g., [delirium tremens](/wiki/Delirium_tremens "Delirium tremens") and [nausea](/wiki/Nausea "Nausea")) * **[psychological dependence](/wiki/Psychological_dependence "Psychological dependence")** – dependence that is characterised by emotional-motivational withdrawal symptoms (e.g., [anhedonia](/wiki/Anhedonia "Anhedonia") and [anxiety](/wiki/Anxiety "Anxiety")) that affect cognitive functioning. * **[reinforcing](/wiki/Reinforcement "Reinforcement") stimuli** – stimuli that increase the probability of repeating behaviors paired with them * **[rewarding](/wiki/Reward_system "Reward system") stimuli** – stimuli that the brain interprets as intrinsically positive and desirable or as something to approach * **[sensitization](/wiki/Sensitization "Sensitization")** – an amplified response to a stimulus resulting from repeated exposure to it * **[substance use disorder](/wiki/Substance_use_disorder "Substance use disorder")** – a condition in which the use of substances leads to clinically and functionally significant impairment or distress * **[drug tolerance](/wiki/Drug_tolerance "Drug tolerance")** – the diminishing effect of a drug resulting from repeated administration at a given dose | |
| * [v](/wiki/Template:Addiction_glossary "Template:Addiction glossary") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Addiction_glossary "Template talk:Addiction glossary") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Addiction_glossary "Special:EditPage/Template:Addiction glossary") | |

Examples (and [ICD-10](/wiki/ICD-10 "ICD-10") code) of withdrawal syndrome include:

* F10.3 [alcohol withdrawal syndrome](/wiki/Alcohol_withdrawal_syndrome "Alcohol withdrawal syndrome") (which can lead to [delirium tremens](/wiki/Delirium_tremens "Delirium tremens"))
* F11.3 [opioid withdrawal](/wiki/Opioid_withdrawal "Opioid withdrawal"), including [methadone withdrawal](/wiki/Methadone#Withdrawal_symptoms "Methadone")[[9]](#cite_note-9)
* F12.3 [cannabis withdrawal](/wiki/Cannabis_withdrawal "Cannabis withdrawal")
* F13.3 [benzodiazepine withdrawal](/wiki/Benzodiazepine_withdrawal_syndrome "Benzodiazepine withdrawal syndrome")
* F14.3 [cocaine withdrawal](/wiki/Cocaine_dependence#Withdrawal_symptoms "Cocaine dependence")
* F15.3 [caffeine withdrawal](/wiki/Caffeine#Dependence_and_withdrawal "Caffeine")
* F17.3 [nicotine withdrawal](/wiki/Nicotine_withdrawal "Nicotine withdrawal")

## Prescription medicine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drug_withdrawal&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Prescription medicine")]

As noted above, many drugs should not be stopped abruptly[[10]](#cite_note-10) without the advice and supervision of a physician, especially if the medication induces dependence or if the condition they are being used to treat is potentially dangerous and likely to return once medication is stopped, such as [diabetes](/wiki/Diabetes "Diabetes"), [asthma](/wiki/Asthma "Asthma"), [heart conditions](/wiki/Cardiovascular_disease "Cardiovascular disease") and many psychological or neurological conditions, like [epilepsy](/wiki/Epilepsy "Epilepsy"), [depression](/wiki/Depression_(mood) "Depression (mood)"), [hypertension](/wiki/Hypertension "Hypertension"), [schizophrenia](/wiki/Schizophrenia "Schizophrenia") and [psychosis](/wiki/Psychosis "Psychosis"). The stopping of [antipsychotics](/wiki/Antipsychotic "Antipsychotic") in schizophrenia and psychoses needs [monitoring](/wiki/Antipsychotic#Discontinuation "Antipsychotic").[[11]](#cite_note-BMJ-11) The stopping of [antidepressants](/wiki/Antidepressant "Antidepressant") for example, can lead to [antidepressant discontinuation syndrome](/wiki/Antidepressant_discontinuation_syndrome "Antidepressant discontinuation syndrome"). With careful physician attention, however, medication prioritization and discontinuation can decrease costs, simplify prescription regimens, decrease risks of adverse drug events and poly-pharmacy, focus therapies where they are most effective, and prevent cost-related under-use of medications.[[12]](#cite_note-alexander-12)

Medication Appropriateness Tool for Comorbid Health Conditions in Dementia[[13]](#cite_note-13) ([MATCH-D](/wiki/MATCH-D "MATCH-D")) warns that people with dementia are more likely to experience adverse effects, and to monitor carefully for withdrawal symptoms when ceasing medications for these people as they are both more likely to experience symptoms and less likely to be able to reliably report symptoms.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Anti-hypertensive drugs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Drug_withdrawal&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Anti-hypertensive drugs")]

The latest evidence does not have evidence of an effect due to discontinuing vs continuing medications used for treating elevated blood pressure or prevention of heart disease in older adults on all-case mortality and incidence of heart attack.[[15]](#cite_note-:2-15) The findings are based on low quality evidence suggesting it may be safe to stop anti-hypertensive medications. However, older adults should not stop any of their medications without talking to a healthcare professional.[[15]](#cite_note-:2-15)

7. **[^](#cite_ref-Cellular_basis_7-0)** Nestler EJ (December 2013). ["Cellular basis of memory for addiction"](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3898681). *Dialogues in Clinical Neuroscience*. **15** (4): 431–443. [PMC](/wiki/PMC_(identifier) "PMC (identifier)") [3898681](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3898681). [PMID](/wiki/PMID_(identifier) "PMID (identifier)") [24459410](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24459410). "Despite the importance of numerous psychosocial factors, at its core, drug addiction involves a biological process: the ability of repeated exposure to a drug of abuse to induce changes in a vulnerable brain that drive the compulsive seeking and taking of drugs, and loss of control over drug use, that define a state of addiction. ... A large body of literature has demonstrated that such ΔFosB induction in D1-type [nucleus accumbens] neurons increases an animal's sensitivity to drug as well as natural rewards and promotes drug self-administration, presumably through a process of positive reinforcement ... Another ΔFosB target is cFos: as ΔFosB accumulates with repeated drug exposure it represses c-Fos and contributes to the molecular switch whereby ΔFosB is selectively induced in the chronic drug-treated state.41. ... Moreover, there is increasing evidence that, despite a range of genetic risks for addiction across the population, exposure to sufficiently high doses of a drug for long periods of time can transform someone who has relatively lower genetic loading into an addict."
8. **[^](#cite_ref-Brain_disease_8-0)** Volkow ND, Koob GF, McLellan AT (January 2016). ["Neurobiologic Advances from the Brain Disease Model of Addiction"](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6135257). *New England Journal of Medicine*. **374** (4): 363–371. [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1056/NEJMra1511480](https://doi.org/10.1056%2FNEJMra1511480). [PMC](/wiki/PMC_(identifier) "PMC (identifier)") [6135257](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6135257). [PMID](/wiki/PMID_(identifier) "PMID (identifier)") [26816013](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/26816013). "Substance-use disorder: A diagnostic term in the fifth edition of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-5) referring to recurrent use of alcohol or other drugs that causes clinically and functionally significant impairment, such as health problems, disability, and failure to meet major responsibilities at work, school, or home. Depending on the level of severity, this disorder is classified as mild, moderate, or severe.  
   Addiction: A term used to indicate the most severe, chronic stage of substance-use disorder, in which there is a substantial loss of self-control, as indicated by compulsive drug taking despite the desire to stop taking the drug. In the DSM-5, the term addiction is synonymous with the classification of severe substance-use disorder."
